title: Securing hessian to a chair seat
subtitle: Fit and tension the hessian base layer over a webbed seat frame
excerpt: How to cut, position, and staple a hessian base layer over a webbed chair seat frame: positioning the hessian grain square to the frame, tensioning from the centre out on each axis, folding and stapling the corners, and checking the surface is drum-tight before adding the filling layer.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery syllabus.

content.text: Hessian must be fixed drum-tight over the webbing with no movement under hand pressure. A loose
hessian
layer allows the filling above to shift and migrate toward the frame edges under use. The
tack rail
must be clear of old staples and debris before starting.
Step 1, cut the hessian to size

Cut the hessian 50 mm (2 in) larger than the seat platform on all sides; this overhang folds down over the outer face of the tack rail to provide two thicknesses of material under the staples. Cut along a single thread of the weave to ensure the cut is straight and square to the grain. A cut that does not follow the weave introduces a diagonal bias into the fabric that causes it to pull unevenly under tension.
Step 2, position the hessian

Place the hessian grain-square over the seat frame. The weave grid must run parallel to the frame rails, not at a diagonal. Fold 12 mm (1/2 in) of the hessian edge back on itself along all four sides to create a double layer at the fixing point. This double layer grips the staple more securely than a single thickness.
Step 3, staple from the centre out

Drive one temporary staple at the centre of the back rail. Pull the hessian firmly toward you and drive one staple at the centre of the front rail. Move to the sides: one staple at the centre of each side rail. Check the weave is straight across the frame before proceeding. Work out from each centre staple toward the corners in 30 to 40 mm (1 1/4 to 1 1/2 in) increments, maintaining firm, even pull at each point.
Step 4, fold and secure the corners

At each corner, fold the hessian neatly over the corner in a single diagonal mitre fold rather than bunching it. Pull each flap flat and drive two or three staples through both layers close to the corner. Trim any excess hessian that projects beyond the tack rail edge.
Step 5, check tension and fix the final staples

Press the centre of the fixed hessian with a flat hand; it should resist deflection under firm pressure and bounce back when the hand is lifted. If it deflects noticeably, the tension is insufficient: remove the temporary centre staples and re-pull tighter before replacing them with permanent staples. Once tension is correct, drive the permanent staples at the tack rail edge at 30 mm (1 1/4 in) intervals along all four sides.
Common mistakes
Starting at a corner rather than the centre.
Starting at a corner locks in any grain-skew before it can be corrected. Always staple both centre points on an axis before adding any others.
Folding a single thickness of hessian over the rail.
A single thickness at the staple line tears under load. The double fold is essential for long service life.
